coredata.dc:title: New ZnFe2O4@SiO2@graphene quantum dots as an effective nanocarrier for targeted DOX delivery and CT-DNA binder
coredata.dc:creator: Mirzaei-Kalar, Zeinab
Kiani Nejad, Zahra
Khandar, Ali Akbar
coredata.dc:description: The current study pronounces the synthesis and characterization of ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4@SiO2, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles by different techniques such as VSM, XRD, IR, UV–Vis and SEM. For application in targeted drug delivery, then GQDs were immobilized on the surface ZnFe2O4@SiO2 via covalent linkage to surface amino groups.The competence of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles was assessed by loading and releasing of doxorubicin (DOX) as a standard anticancer drug at pH = 5 (pH of cancerous cell) and pH = 7.4 (pH of healthy cell). The attained results authenticate the pH sensitive release behavior of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles by releasing more DOX at cancerous pH (70%) than healthy pH (28%). To appraise the biological manner, ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles were screened for their CT-DNA binding tendency by means of spectroscopic methods. The gained results of absorption spectroscopy and competitive fluorescence studies disclosed that nanoparticles bind to DNA through the intercalation mode and the loading of DOX on the nanocarrier has increased CT-DNA binding affinity up to 300 times. The cytotoxicity activity of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX by MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay revealed on cancerous HeLa cell and healthy HEK-293 cell revealed that both nanoparticles exhibit concentration-dependent behavior on both cell lines, so that with increasing nanoparticle concentration, the cell viability percentage decreases, although nanocarriers containing DOX expression a more severe effect. Treatment of HeLa cells with 10 µg/mL of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles showed that the nanoparticles induce cell death by apoptosis and cause detention of cell cycle in G2 phase. The results of the study expression the potential of the nanocarrier for targeted and controlled drug delivery, which should be given more attention and modified as a nanocarrier.

head.ce:title: New ZnFe2O4@SiO2@graphene quantum dots as an effective nanocarrier for targeted DOX delivery and CT-DNA binder

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study pronounces the synthesis and characterization of ZnFe2O4, ZnFe2O4@SiO2, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles by different techniques such as VSM, XRD, IR, UV–Vis and SEM. For application in targeted drug delivery, then GQDs were immobilized on the surface ZnFe2O4@SiO2 via covalent linkage to surface amino groups.The competence of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles was assessed by loading and releasing of doxorubicin (DOX) as a standard anticancer drug at pH = 5 (pH of cancerous cell) and pH = 7.4 (pH of healthy cell). The attained results authenticate the pH sensitive release behavior of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles by releasing more DOX at cancerous pH (70%) than healthy pH (28%). To appraise the biological manner, ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles were screened for their CT-DNA binding tendency by means of spectroscopic methods. The gained results of absorption spectroscopy and competitive fluorescence studies disclosed that nanoparticles bind to DNA through the intercalation mode and the loading of DOX on the nanocarrier has increased CT-DNA binding affinity up to 300 times. The cytotoxicity activity of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX by MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay revealed on cancerous HeLa cell and healthy HEK-293 cell revealed that both nanoparticles exhibit concentration-dependent behavior on both cell lines, so that with increasing nanoparticle concentration, the cell viability percentage decreases, although nanocarriers containing DOX expression a more severe effect. Treatment of HeLa cells with 10 µg/mL of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles showed that the nanoparticles induce cell death by apoptosis and cause detention of cell cycle in G2 phase. The results of the study expression the potential of the nanocarrier for targeted and controlled drug delivery, which should be given more attention and modified as a nanocarrier.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The steady increase in cancer complications and mortality rates requires highly effective cancer treatment strategies. Chemotherapy usually fails to cure cancer or prolong survival due to its toxicity to normal cells, side effects, and lack of targeting. Thus, nanotechnology is emerging as a hopeful means for reducing the above side effects and effective treatment in cancer chemotherapy. In recent decades, nanoscale drug carriers with multi-mode imaging capabilities, targeted drug delivery, diagnosis and simultaneous treatment have attracted much attention in various medical fields. Drug nanocarriers significantly improve therapeutic efficacy with minimal side effects with early diagnosis and treatment [1]. Among the various candidates nanocarriers of chemotherapy agents, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a significant topic for extensive research, and related therapeutic approaches have shown great potential in the treatment of cancer [2,3]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) show optimum biocompatibility with low toxicity, outstanding physical features, a changeable surface, and compatibility with conventional graphene technology.

In particular, the usage of graphene derivatives for an extensive variety of biological applications, including the new growth of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based biomarkers for tumor imaging, has been very promising. Recently shown that graphene-based materials with aromatic ring-containing anticancer drugs such as doxorubicin (DOX) and camptothesin (CPT) can be loaded with extremely high-efficiency [4].

Different types of nanomaterials have been effectively employed to develop sophisticated diagnosis and therapy techniques. In this context, graphene and its derivatives e.g. graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) have been inspected as talented candidates to design graphene-based nanosystems for the diagnosis and therapeutic purpose [5–8]. Several physical and chemical methods to synthesize graphene and its derivatives (e.g GO, RGO) have been well recognized. Further, a number of techniques are being employed to produce graphene and its derivatives following bottom-up [9] and top-down [10] approach. In this reference, methods e.g. multi-roll milling [11], electrochemical exfoliation [12], microwave assisted plasma decomposition [13], chemical-vapor deposition [14], UV-mediated hydrothermal [15], epitaxial growth [16], chemical reduction [17,18], have been studied. Nevertheless, these methods exhibit certain advantages and disadvantages. And therefore, to achieve a stable dispersion and scalability to produce graphene and its derivatives still one of the major issues for their industrial applications [19]. Current works have exposed that graphene derivatives have outstanding catalytic act due to their high surface area and polar interactions with oxygen-containing functional groups which is an important factor in increasing drug loading capacity. Many different hydrophobic drugs, such as curcumin, are very effective in contradiction of numerous cancers. However, the low solubility of these hydrophobic drugs in aqueous media precludes their clinical use for treatment. Surajit et al., due to the properties of graphene used drug nanocomposites based on DGO, graphene oxide (GO), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as new nanocarriers for the delivery of the hydrophobic drug anticancer curcumin [20]. Magnetic nanocarriers based on magnetite nanoparticles have attracted much attention due to ease of surface modification and high biological compatibility in the medical field [21]. Liang et al., encapsulated GQDS or methylene blue (MB) together with DOX into the cores of poly lactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA) nanoparticles coated with bovine serum albumin (BSA) based on the emulsion method. The in vitro chemophotothermal synergetic tests demonstrated that GQDs@DOX/PB nanoparticles have a high tumor cell-killing effectiveness, displaying outstanding potential in synergistic therapy [22]. Zhang et al., prepared GQDs conjugated to polyethylene glycol which unveiled low cytotoxicity, good solution stability and outstanding endocytosis. Both in vitro and in vivo results confirmed strong ablation for oral squamous cell carcinoma under irradiation [23].

Since deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is the most important cellular molecule and is the primary target for many anticancer drugs, it is undoubtedly the most important target for drug design and development [24]. Notwithstanding the severe cytotoxicity of the most successful anticancer drugs, their therapeutic effects are achieved by targeting DNA. Small molecules can bind to DNA in both covalent (such as cis platin, carboplatin and oxaliplatin [25]) and non-covalent modes (such as DOX [26]) that due to its lower toxicity, the non-covalent bonding mode is safer [27]. Efforts are now being made to targeted deliver non-covalently CT-binders to cancerous tissues to minimize cytotoxicity to healthy tissues as much as possible. In this regard, in the present study, after evaluation DOX loading on the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanocarrier and release efficiency in a pH-dependent manner, its interaction with CT-DNA was examined by multi-spectroscopic methods. The cytotoxicity activity was studied by MTT method on Hell cancer cell and HEK-293 normal cell after treatment with various concentrations of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles. Apoptosis and cell cycle studies were done on HeLa cell after treatment with 10 µg/mL of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles.

The following compounds were used for the synthesis of the samples: ZnCl2, FeCl3 6H2O, glycerol, CH3COONa aqueous solution of ammonia and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) which all were prepared from Merck Chemical Company. Tris-HCl buffer, calf thymus DNA (CT-DNA, Hoechst 33258 and ethidium bromide (EtBr) were prepared from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Company. Doxorubicin (DOX) was also purchased from Exir Nano Sina Company (Tehran, Iran). Cancerous HeLa cell lines (human cervix carcinoma) and HEK-293 normal cell lines (human embryonic kidney) were provided from the Pasteur Institute of Iran (Tehran, Iran).

FT-IR spectrum of samples with Bruker Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (for identification of functional groups), magnetism of samples with vibrating sample magnetometer with VSM LBKFB, absorption spectra with Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer, emission spectra with Shimadzu RF-540 fluorescence spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan) and powder XRD pattern with PHILIPS PW1730 were recorded. Morphology study and determination of sample constituents was performed with scanning electron microscope (SEM TESCAN model MIRA III) equipped with EDX (energy dispersive X-ray) analysis system. The emission spectra were collected using a Shimadzu RF-540 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a quartz cell (1 cm × 1 cm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4@SiO2 magnetic nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoparticles were synthesized according to our previous report [28]. Briefly: ZnCl2 (0.001 mol) and FeCl3·6H2O (0.002 mol)) were dissolved in deionized water and glycerol to ratio. After stirring for 30 min at ambient temperature, CH3COONa (0.0097 mol) was added to the resulting mixture under vigorous stirring. The obtained solution after transferring to autoclave 50 ml was heated for 12 h at 200 °C. The resulting black precipitate after separation by centrifugation, was washed several times with water and ethanol to remove unreacted materials.

Then, black powder ZnFe2O4 (2 mmol) in deionized/ethanol water for 15 min was dispersed by ultrasonic. Then, TEOS (6.27 mmol) and NH4OH added to the suspension and the mixture was stirred with a mechanical stirrer for 20 h at 60 °C under atmosphere N2. The precipitate was separate by centrifugation and after five times washed with ethanol dried in a vacuum desiccator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles functionalized with 3-aminopropyl trimethoxy silane (APTMS)
ce:section.ce:para: 250 mg (83 mg) from ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles coated with silica was dispersed in 40 ml of absolute ethanol by ultrasonic. The 75 ml (4.3 mmol) of APTM was added drop wise to the obtained suspension. In the next step, the sample was refluxed for 24 h at 80 °C under N2 atmosphere. The precipitate collected by centrifugation for remove APTMS unreacted was washed several times with ethanol and dried in a vacuum dryer at room temperature. The precipitate collected by centrifuge to remove APTMS unreacted was washed several times with ethanol and dried in the vacuum desiccator at room temperature.

Citric acid pyrolysis was used to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [29]. Briefly, beaker 20 ml containing 2 g citric acid were heated in an oven at 200 °C for 30 min. After 10 min, the citric acid liquefied and gradually the color of the liquid changed from colorless to pale yellow and finally to orange. Then, the solution of quantum dots was obtained by adding the obtained orange liquid to the NaOH solution under stirring and adjusting the pH of the solution to 7. Excess solvent was removed by vacuum distillation and obtained precipitation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

The ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanocarrier was prepared by the following method: First, 1000 mg graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were dissolved in 100 ml of PBS buffer (10 mg/ml in). Then, 1600 mg of EDC and 200 mg NHS added to the solution and the sample was stirred for 30 min in the dark. In the final step, 20 mg of ZnFe2O4@SiO2-NH2 nanoparticles were added to the solution and the 24 h mixture was stirred in environment temperature. The sample of centrifuge and to remove excess NHS was washed several times with PBS buffer. The resulting precipitate was dried in a vacuum desiccator.

To measure quantum yield, quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 with a quantum yield of 0.54 was used as standard. First, the emission of standard and main samples was measured at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, then the absorption of the main and standard samples was read at the same wavelength. Finally, using equation (1) quantum yield was calculated.

Generally, 15 mg of DOX drug was mixed with 15 mg nanoparticles of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs in 6 ml PBS buffer (pH = 4.7). After 24 h stirring at room temperature, the sample was centrifuged and precipitate dried in a vacuum desiccator. The concentration of free DOX was determined by measuring the adsorption of the supernatant at 487 nm. The capacity of the loaded DOX was calculated using following equation:

15 mg of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles were dispersed in 3 ml of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 5.5 and 7.4) and stirred in a dark environment inside an incubator shaker at 37°C to evaluate in vitro DOX release from nanocarrier. To measure the DOX release at each time interval, first 2 ml of the solution was removed and after centrifugation for 15 min at 12000 rpm, the supernatant was placed in UV cell and after measuring the amount of dox released, the supernatant was returned to the main container. The DOX release study was repeated three times and the presented data are the average of the obtained results.

The percentage of the released DOX was determined via the obtained absorbance in the UV–Vis spectrum of the sampled solution, calibration curve, and Eq. (3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biophysical studies to determination CT-DNA binding nature of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: For spectroscopic studies to determine the CT-DNA binding mode of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles, stock solutions were first prepared as follows:

By dissolving an appropriate amount of CT-DNA in the Tris-HCl buffer, the stock solution was prepared and its concentration was calculated using the molar absorption coefficient at 260 nm (ɛ260 = 6600 M−1cm−1). Meanwhile, the stock of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles was prepared by ultrasonic dispersion of the appropriate amount in the Tris-HCl buffer.

In order to obtain information about the nature of CT-DNA binding of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles by UV method, solutions with constant concentration of nanoparticles (5 × 10−5 mol. L−1) were prepared and with gradual increase of CT-DNA concentration (from 0 to 5 × 10−5 mol. L−1), UV spectrum was measured.

To determine more precisely of binding mode, competitive fluorescence studies were performed with two well-known CT-DNA binders as probe, the well-known DNA intercalator EtBr and the well-known Hoechst 33258 groove-binder. In this study, the concentrations of CT-DNA and probes were kept constant at 5 × 10−5 and 5 × 10−6 mol. L−1, respectively, while the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticle concentrations were changed from 0 to 5 × 10−4 g. ml−1 to investigate the ability of nanoparticles to probes replacement. To record emission spectra for Hoechst-DNA complex, the samples were excited at 340 nm and fluorescence spectra measured in the range of 460 to 620 nm, and for EtBr-DNA complex, the spectra were recorded in the range of 550 to 700 nm when excited at 525 nm.

To obtain more valuable details about the binding nature of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles to CT-DNA, fluorescence emission spectrum changes of nanoparticles were measured in the absence and presence of CT-DNA. For this purpose, the concentration of nanoparticles was kept constant at 5 × 10−5 mol. L−1, while the concentration of CT-DNA changed from 0 to 2.5 × 10−5 mol. L−1. Also, to calculation thermodynamic data and understanding the nature of the involved forces in the interaction, these studies were performed at three temperatures of 288, 298 and 308 K.

To appraise the cytotoxicity effect of nanocarrier, the cytotoxicity of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles was explored against HeLa cancer cell line and HEK-293 normal cells by the MTT assay (tetrazolium salt reduction). The cells were cultured in 96-well plates at 2 × 105 well/cells with diverse amounts of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles (3.125–100 µg/mL) in a high humidity condition with 5% CO2 at 310 K for 48 h. After the treatment, 200 µL of MTT solution (2 mg mL−1 in phosphate buffers) was surcharged to each well and the mixture was incubated for 4 h. The medium with MTT was removed and made formazan crystals were dissolved by addition an applicable amount of dimethyl sulfoxide. The 96-well plate was read by an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader at 570 nm for computation of optical density to assess the cell viability. The MTT assays were repeated three times and data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apoptosis induction and cell cycle studies
ce:section.ce:para: Appraisement of the progress of apoptosis in Hela cell after treatment of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles was accomplished by Annexin V-FITC and propidium iodide (PI) kit (Invitrogen, USA). The cells were harvested after 24 h treatment with 10 µg/mL of the nanoparticles and centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 5 min. Afterward, the cells washed twice with cold phosphate buffer saline (PBS), and finally resuspended in the supplied Annexin V binding buffer. At that time, both Annexin V-PE and 7-AAD were added to the solution and incubated in the dark for 15 min at 25 °C. Results were investigated using a BD Biosciences FACSVerse flow cytometer within 1 h of sample preparation.

For investigation the cell cycle arrest by the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles, the HeLa cells were grown in 24-well plates and treated with 10 µg/mL of the nanocarrier for 48 h. After centrifugation and remove the medium, the cells were cooled with PBS and fixed with 70% ethanol. The cells were stored at −20 °C until analysis. To evaluate the cell cycle steps, control and treated samples were mixed with 20 mg propidium iodine (PI) and 20 mg RNase (dissolved in 1 ml PBS) for 30 min and analyzed by flow cytometry (Partec, German).

The magnetism of the synthesized samples was investigation by vibrating sample magnetometer technique. The magnetic curves in Fig. 1 a show that the magnetism of ZnFe2O4 and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs is equal to 75/44 emu−1 and 30/44 emu−1, respectively. Silica coating around the primary nanoparticles and surface modification reduces the magnetic properties of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs.

Size and morphology of primary ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As Fig. 1b can be seen, the spherical particles and their average size is 19 nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed to investigate the structure and morphology of the GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nano platform (Fig. 1c). From the TEM image, it can be seen that coating of the GQDs onto the surface of ZnFe2O4@SiO2 nanospheres was achieved through acylamide binding in the presence of EDC as the activator. As shown in Fig. 1c, the resulting nanostructure were spherical nanoparticles and GQDs are present at the periphery of nanospheres.

The FT-IR spectrum of ZnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles, GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs are shown in Fig. 1d. In the FT-IR spectrum of silica-coated ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles, bands related to vibrations Si–O–Si and vibrations Si-OH appear at 799.44 cm−1, 1095.05 cm−1 and 987.51 cm−1, respectively. In the IR spectrum of citric acid, vibrating bands in 772/32 cm−1, 1143/53 cm−1, 1701/24 cm−1, 1748/99 cm−1, 3295/20 cm−1 and 2564/54 cm−1-2648/33 cm−1 are attributed to the functional groups C-OH, C-H2, C=O, O-H and C-H, respectively. Also, in the IR spectrum, the GQDs synthesized the peaks observed in 1072/51 cm−1, 1261.58 cm−1 and 3026/84 cm−1, 1391/75 cm−1 and 1562/73 cm−1, 3418.74 cm−1 are referred to the Stretching vibrations C-OH of hydroxyl functional group, bending and Stretching vibrations of C-H bond (C-H bond represents incomplete carbonation of citric acid), symmetric and asymmetric Stretching vibrations of COO− and H-O, respectively [31]. As can be seen in IR spectrum the of Fig. 2 d, the bands related to the Fe-O and Zn-O bond vibrations appearing at 571/331 cm−1 and 433.80 cm−1, respectively. The ν (C-H aliphatic) band in the spectra of APTMS at 2933 cm−1 shifted to 2925/61 cm−1, which indicates the successful binding of APTMS on the surface of ZnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. Also, after covalently coupling ZnFe2O4@SiO2 to GQDs, the band observed in 1394/01 cm−1 is attributed to C-N vibrations and the peaks of 1569/45 cm−1 and 1638/34 cm−1 are assigned to -CO-NH- vibrations which indicates the formation of an amide bond in ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs.

GQDs have a strong absorption peak in the ultraviolet region [32]. Therefore, UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to inspect the absorption properties of GQDs. The absorption bands observed at 241 nm and 351 nm in Fig. 2a belong to the n-π⁎ and π-π⁎ transitions of the C=O functional group and C=C, respectively [33].

The emission property of GQDs is related to the functional groups on the surface and the edge. The emission spectrum reported in Fig. 2a show, λem, max of GQDs in water solvent in excitation wavelength 360 nm is equal to 460 nm [34].

To further investigate the formation of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs from samples synthesized were taken UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 2b). GQDs do show the maximum absorption at 365 nm. ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles and silica-coated ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles do not show clear adsorption band. After binding of ZnFe2O4@SiO2-NH2 to the GQDs, the absorption spectrum of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs shows the characteristic band of GQDs at 325 nm, indicating the successful formation of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanocarrier.

Also, the emission spectrum of GQDs, ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@ GQDs/DOX are shown in Fig. 2b. As can be seen in the Fig, GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs show the maximum emission at 460 nm DOX loading on the GQDs surface by pi-pi interaction cause energy transfer from GQDs to DOX, GQDs fluorescence signal off and the appearance of the emission peak by DOX at 600 nm.

Quantum yield of GQDs changes from 24% to 15% when bounded to nanoparticles due to the occurrence of fluorescence quenching. A similar behavior has been previously reported for PEI-GQDs upon coordination to the Pc-Au@Ag [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of FRET mechanism based on ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanocarrier and calculation of detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 , the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanocarrier is composed of three components: 1) the core-shell structure of ZnFe2O4@SiO2, 2) GQDs, with formation covalent bond between the carboxyl groups of GQDs and the amine groups present on the nanoparticle surface, the GQDs bind to ZnFe2O4@SiO2 and the resulting nanocarrier has a dual function, acting as donor molecule in FRET and a fluorescence probe, 3) DOX, which binds to GQDs by pi-pi interaction and has a dual function due to its fluorescent properties, acts as acceptor molecule in FRET and anticancer drug.

The combination of these components together forms a FRET system, so that the DOX adsorption on the GQDs surface turns off the GQDs fluorescence property. After nanocarrier uptake by cancer cells, in the acidic environment of cancer cells, DOX was released from the surface of GQDs and the fluorescence property of GQDs is activated. GQDs at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm show the maximum emission at the 460 nm wavelength; the excitation wavelength of DOX as a fluorescent anticancer drug is 480 nm, the overlap of the DOX excitation spectrum and the GQDs emission spectrum forms the FRET system between DOX and GQDs. The emission spectrum of GQDs, ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@ GQDs/DOX are shown in Fig. 3 a. As can be seen in the Fig, GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs show the maximum emission at 460 nm DOX loading on the GQDs surface by pi-pi interaction cause energy transfer from GQDs to DOX, GQDs fluorescence signal off and the appearance of the emission peak by DOX at 600 nm.

To evaluate the FRET system, the concentration of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs was kept constant at 5 × 10−5 M though the amounts of DOX changed from 0 to 2 × 10−5 M (ri = 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.7, 0.8,1, 1.2, 1.5, 2). Increasing the DOX concentration caused a continuous increase in the DOX emission spectrum and continuous decrease in the GQDs emission spectrum. Observation of this behavior indicates an increase in the adsorption of DOX molecules on surface GQDs (Fig. 3a). FRET is a distance-sensitive ‘smart system’ for drug delivery, biosensing, and bioimaging applications. In a FRET system, energy is transferred from a donor molecule to acceptor molecule. The energy transfer reduces the fluorescence intensity of the donor and increases the fluorescent emission of the acceptor molecule. FRET is used for measuring the distance between donor and acceptor and it is employed to study biological interactions. Various nanoparticles such as quantum dots, mesoporous silica nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles, etc. have been used to develop a FRET system [36]. GQDs-based FRET system is designed for drug delivery with doxorubicin nuclear target. GQDs act as doxorubicin carriers and donors of FRET pairs and were conjugated to the TAT peptide to target the nucleus. The release of doxorubicin can be controlled in real time using this system [37]. Therefore, theoretically, the FRET mechanism was expected for this donor and receptor, which was confirmed by the practical results.

According to obtained fluorescence spectra, the detection limits of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDS were resolved based on the LOD = 3σ/S, Where LOD represents the detection limit of the probe; σ is the criterion of the blank solution, which S is regarded as the slope of linear equation of the DOX fluorescence titration of sensors [38]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity declined gradually at 410 nm with the increase of DOX concentrations and the reduction was down to 103 folds. In a certain range of DOX concentrations from 35 to 60 μM, there existed a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs at 410 nm and DOX concentration (R2 = 0.9986) (Fig. 3b). The calculated detection limit was 43 nM under the experimental conditions, which could be concluded that probe ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs was very sensitive to determine DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading and release study to ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The potential use of nanoparticles as a drug delivery system was appraised by loading DOX (as an anticancer drug). The results of UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 3c) show clearly reduction of DOX concentration in aqueous solution after loading. This behavior demonstrated that more than 99% of the drug adsorbed on the surface of nanoparticles and affirmed the existence of a strong interaction between nanoparticles and the drug.

Fig. 3d depicts the release profiles of Dox from the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanocarriers at pH values of 7.4 and 5. pH = 7.4 and 5 mimics the physiological pH of normal cells and endosomal pH of tumor cells, respectively. The outcomes illustration that DOX releasing from the nanocarrier occurs via a controlled and time/pH-dependent manner. At both pHs, the rate of drug release is higher in the first 9 h (24% and 58% for pH 7.4 and 5, respectively) and then the release rate slows down until it reaches plateau after 72 h with 28% and 70% release for pH 7.4 and 5, respectively. This means that there is a relatively strong interaction between the drug molecules and the nanoparticle surface at pH 7.4, which allows only weakly bounded molecules to the nanoparticle surface to be released. The increase in drug release rate by lowering the pH from 7.4 to 5 can be elucidated by the fact that at neutral pH there is a strong hydrogen bond between the nanoparticles and the drug, which causes the drug molecules to remain on the surface of the nanoparticles. Decreasing the pH weakens the bond and results in more drug release in the acidic pH. This observation is consistent with formerly described work on Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-Glu nanoparticles [39,40].

On the other hand, according to our previous report (releasing drug from ZnFe2O4@SiO2-DOX nanoparticles) [28], it is clear that by changing the nanocarrier from ZnFe2O4@SiO2 to ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs, the drug release rate increases, although the drug release amount at acidic pH (from 57% to 70%) is more than neutral pH (from 25% to 28%). The obtained data show that by modifying the nanocarriers, the controlled release amounts of the drug can be increased. In addition to releasing the drug, the metal may also leak into the environment and cause contamination. By forming an inert layer of silica on the surface of zinc oxide nanoparticles, its aggregation in solution is prevented and its chemical stability is improved [41]. Therefore, it can be said that there is practically no possibility of metal leaking into the solution and only the drug is released over time. Therefore, ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs system can be considered as a promising nanocarrier for targeted drug delivery.

Most biomolecular activities are monitored in soluble medium, so the test compounds must be stable in biological media to produce accurate activity data in various cell-based assays [42]. Drug release studies showed that drug release is time dependent and since the study of solution phase stability is an important measure in the development of new drugs, therefore, before CT-DNA binding study of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles, the stability of the nanoparticle solution phase in the Tris-HCl buffer was investigated and the results are presented in Fig. S1 [Because the nanoparticles were incubated with DNA for one hour, stability studies were controlled over a period of 2 h by electronic absorption spectra]. As the results show, a small percentage of these drug molecules (only 3%) are released, representative their suitability for biological studies.

After ensuring the sufficient stability of nanoparticles in Tris-HCl buffer, the next step is to investigate CT-DNA interaction of nanoparticles. Among from different available techniques for DNA binding study, UV–Visible is the most useful of them. When a small molecule binds to DNA and form a complex, it makes changes in the structure and conformation of DNA that are reflected in spectral changes. The extent of binding may be considered to be associated with the degree of alterations in the absorbance (Hyperchromic and hypochromic effects) and shifting in the peak position (blue or red shift) [43].

The absorption spectra of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs-DOX nanoparticles were followed at constant concentrations (5 × 10−5 mol. L−1) in the absence and in the presence of incremental adding in CT-DNA concentration (from 0 to 5 × 10−5 mol. L−1) in the region of 350–750 nm in Tric-HCl buffer, and the changes in absorption profile are presented in Fig. 4 a. Inspection in absorption profiles of nanoparticles reveals that with stepwise addition of CT-DNA hypochromic effect occurs without any blue or red shift. The experiential hypochromic effect for ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs-DOX nanoparticles was 34%. Since the effect is observed when aromatic compounds are inserted between base pairs of DNA [44], it can be concluded that nanoparticles are inserted between DNA bases, and this is a reason for the intercalating binding mode.

The binding constant was calculated according to the following equation to obtain the binding strength of the nanoparticles to the CT-DNA [28]:

ɛa, ɛf, and ɛb represent extinguish coefficients for the absorption band at assumed amounts of CT-DNA, the free compound, and when fully bound to the CT-DNA, respectively. Kb, Ct and s are the equilibrium binding constant, the total compound concentration in the nucleotide and the binding site size, respectively.

The computed binding constant for ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs-DOX nanoparticles from plot 4.b was 12.08 × 105 L.mol−1. Whereas in earlier works, the binding constant for free DOX and DOX loaded on Fe3O4 and ZnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles on 0.04 × 105, 0.68 × 105 and 1.04 × 105 was obtained, respectively [45,28]. According to these data, it was found that the loading of DOX on Fe3O4 nanoparticles increased the binding constant 17-fold, and by changing the nanocarrier from iron to ZnFe2O4@SiO2, it increased to 26-fold. With the modification of nanocarriers from ZnFe2O4@SiO2 to ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs-DOX, the value has now increased remarkably to 300 times. Based on these results, it can be concluded that by designing and stepwise modification of nanocarriers, the CT-DNA binding affinity of DOX can be increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive probe displacement assay using EtBr and Hoechst 33258
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the binding mode concluded from the absorption spectroscopy studies, two EtBr and Hoechst 33258 probes with the known intercalation and groove binding modes, respectively were used. This experiment can be defensible by the fact that if a molecule can replace a probe in the DNA-probe complex, it certainly has the same binding mode as the probe for binding to DNA [46]. Therefore, by progressively addition of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles to the DNA-probe system using Hoechst 33258 and EtBr, the binding mode of the nanoparticles to the DNA will be specified. The DNA-probe fluorescence emission spectrum profile was recorded by piecemeal increasing the nanoparticles in Tris buffer at room temperature. Fig. 5 a, b demonstrations the changes in the emission spectrum of Hoechst-DNA and EtBr-DNA after the addition of nanoparticles, respectively.

As can be seen from the figure, nanoparticles addition does not cause a significant change in the intensity of the Hoechst-DNA spectrum, but the intensity of the emission spectrum of ethidium bromide-DNA diminished dramatically.

Calculation of the percentage of displacement using the following equation shows that the percentage of Hoechst displacement from Hoechst-DNA and EtBr from EtBr-DNA complex by ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles were 9% and 65%, respectively [28].
(5)

F0 and F in the equation represent the fluorescence intensity of the probe-DNA complex in the absence and the presence of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles, respectively. These data conclusively state that nanoparticles are bound to CT-DNA via an intercalative binding mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature dependent studies for investigation details of CT-DNA binding of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs-DOX nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: As regards fluorescence spectroscopy is a very sensitive technique, to get profound insight and more details about CT-DNA binding of nanoparticles, fluorescence temperature dependent experiments were done. Fluorescence experiments were performed at three temperatures of 288, 298 and 308 K at a constant concentration of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs-DOX nanoparticles (5 × 10−5 mol. L−1) while the concentration of CT-DNA (0–2.5 × 10−5 mol. L−1) steadily increased. The emission spectra of the solutions were recorded in the region of 500–700 nm when the nanoparticles were excited at 450 nm (Fig. 5c) at 288 K. By scrutinize of figure, it is understandable that with increasing CT-DNA concentration, the intensity of emission decreases (quenching) and this trend is observed in the other two temperatures (Fig. S2), but to a greater extent. As the temperature increases from 288 to 298 and 308, the quenching amounts increases from 60% to 66% and 70%, respectively. This manner specifies that some interactions befall between nanoparticles and CT-DNA.

The fluorescence quenching process can be analyzed by Stern–Volmer equation (6) to determine type of quenching mechanism [47]:
(6)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K q
τ
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]

F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and in the presence of quencher (Q), respectively. As well as, Kq and Ksv express the fluorophore quencher constant and the Stern-Volmer constant, respectively. Also, τ is the lifetime of the fluorophore and [Q] is the concentration of the added quencher (CT-DNA). The Stern-Volmer constant calculated from the slope of F0/F vs [Q], and the values are listed in Table 1 and Fig. 5d. Now, according to these data, the type of mechanism can be determined.

There are two general types of known quenching mechanisms, dynamic and static, which are temperature dependent and can be distinguished by temperature changes. Thus, if the Stern-Volmer constants increase with increasing temperature, the mechanism is dynamic, and if the opposite happens, the mechanism is static [48]. And it should be noted that in the dynamic mechanism, the obtained Kq should not be greater than 2 × 1010 L.mol−1s−1 [49], if it is greater than the value, despite Kq increasing with temperature increasing, the dynamic type will not be allowed and will be static. With these interpretations and according to the obtained data, the quenching in the interaction between the nanoparticles and CT-DNA is done through a static mechanism. Static quenching mechanism means that a complex is formed between nanoparticles and DNA, which is consistent with the results of previous sections.

After confirming the formation of the complex between nanoparticles and DNA, the formation constant, Kf, as well as the number of binding sites, n, can be calculated via equation 7 [50]:
(7)

The obtained Kf and n in three different temperatures by plotting log (
F 0

) vs. log [Q] are given in Table 1. The data show that at room temperature the complex consists of a 1:1 ratio of nanoparticles and DNA also, the formation constant obtained is 17.74 × 104 which is well consistent with the results obtained from electronic absorption experiment (12.08 × 105). Comparing the present results with the data of the previous study [28], it is clear that the modification of nanocarriers from ZnFe2O4@SiO2 to ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs increases the tendency of nanoparticles containing DOX to bind to CT-DNA.

To determine the nature of the forces involved in the interaction of nanoparticles with CT-DNA, thermodynamic parameters were computed using the van't-Hoff equation (8, 9). Obtained positive value for ΔS and negative ΔH indicate the preponderance of electrostatic forces in the interaction, and the spontaneity of the interaction is affirmed by the negative ΔG [51,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity studies on cancerous and normal cells
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehend the cytotoxicity effect of synthesized nanocarriers for the design of targeted drug delivery system, the cytotoxicity of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles on cancerous Hela cell and normal HEK-293 cell at different concentrations (3.125–100 μg/mL) was appraised by MTT assay and the results are illustrated in Fig. 6 and the IC50 values are listed in Table 2 . The achieved results demonstrate that in the presence of the nanocarrier the percentage of cell viability decreases, although the amounts % cell viability is significantly decreased by the loading of the anti-cancer drug DOX, also the amounts of % cell viability show concentration-dependent behavior. As the concentration of nanoparticles increases, the amounts of % cell viability decreases, so that at a concentration of 100 μg/mL by DOX loading, the percentage of cell viability is reduced from 25% to 4%.

A similar behavior was observed for normal HEK-293 cells. That is, by loading DOX on the nanocarrier ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQD, the % cell viability at all concentrations is reduced. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, the percentage of cell viability decreases from 38% to 4.6% after DOX loading on nanocarrier. DOX loading, although it inhibits cancer cells growth very well, but also inhibits healthy cells growth. It seems that modifications can be made to the nanocarrier so that the nanocarrier containing anticancer drug is delivered to the cancerous tissue so that it can inhibit the cancer cells while not harming the healthy cells or having the least effect on the healthy cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apoptotic mode of cell death and cell cycle study
ce:section.ce:para: To attend whether the effect of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQD/DOX capability on HeLa cells was induced by infusion of apoptosis, cells were treated with Annexin V-FITC and PI dye for 48 h after treatment with the nanoparticles and the attained results are portrayed in Fig. 7 . As revealed in Fig. 7, when cells were exposed to a concentration of 10 μg/ml ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQD@/DOX, the percentage of cells in the late stages of the apoptotic phase increased from 1.29% in the control sample to 28.2%. That is, the nanocarrier induces apoptosis in Hella cancer cells.

Fig. 7 illustrations the effect of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQD/DOX nanoparticles on cell cycle detention of HeLa cells after treatment with the nanoparticles for 48 h. Inspection of results confirmation that the nanoparticles increased the proportions of HeLa cells at the G2/S phase and decreased the proportions of HeLa cells at the G1 phase. So, the data show that the nanocarrier caused the cell line to arrest at the G2 phase.

Targeted drug delivery can significantly reduce the unwanted side effects of anticancer drugs to healthy tissue through controlled drug secretion and drug use of choice. Therefore, designing a nanoscale drug carrier to increase the preferential accumulation of cancer drugs in cancer tissues is a promising approach to cancer treatment. GQDs as a new promising drug carrier have excellent properties for this purpose. Here, ZnFe2O4@SiO2, GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles research was synthesized and characterized by different techniques such as IR, XRD, SEM, VSM and UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. DOX was then loaded onto ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs nanoparticles as a standard anticancer drug to test the ability of the nanoparticle to act as a nanocarrier in targeted drug delivery and its release was scrutinized at pH 7.4 and pH 5. The results of drug release studies exposed that the amounts of drug release at pH = 5 (pH of cancerous cell), 70%, is much higher than pH = 7.4 (pH of health cell), 28%. This amount is even higher than the amount of drug released in acidic pH on the ZnFe2O4@SiO2 nanoparticles (57%) [28]. That is, conjugating ZnFe2O4@SiO2 platform to graphene quantum dots has increased targeted drug release. Therefore, ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs can be further explored as a potentially powerful drug delivery system. In vitro CT-DNA binding studies of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX under physiological conditions were appraised by spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectroscopy.

Spectroscopic and competitive fluorescence experiments affirmed that ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles bind to CT-DNA via intercalation. As well as it was found that loading DOX onto ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs increases the drug binding affinity to CT-DNA by up to 300 times compared to free drug [23]. The cytotoxicity of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles on Hela cancer line and healthy HEK-293 cell line was determined by MTT method in order to cytotoxicity determination of nanocarriers. The results showed that on both healthy and cancerous cell lines, concentration-dependent behavior is observed so that with increasing the concentration of ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs and ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles, the percentage of cell viability decreases. However, the behavior is more noticeable for ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanocarriers. The finding results on HeLa cells with 10 µg/mL of the ZnFe2O4@SiO2@GQDs/DOX nanoparticles designate that the nanoparticles induce cell death by apoptosis and cause detention of cell cycle in G2 phase. Overall, controlled release of drug carried by GQDs based drug delivery system can improve drug performance by preventing their destruction before reaching the target and increasing uptake at the target. In this regard, modification of these systems and overcome their problems will certainly reduce the unwanted side effects of the drug on healthy cells. On the other hand, despite the results of this research and significant advances and proven benefits of graphene quantum dots, some of the problems associated with these systems have not yet been resolved and the work is done in a laboratory scale. For example, although quantum dot graphene can be produced in a variety of ways, most of these methods still suffer in cases such as nanoparticle synthesis with narrow particle size distributions and non-reproducibility. Most of these reported methods usually suffer from long reaction times, toxic organic solvents, etc., and offer very low product yields. Therefore, the synthesis of these materials on an industrial scale and the reproducibility of these methods should be the first priority so that later, with the modifications that can be made on the surface of these nanoparticles, the required goals in various fields of medicine can be achieved.

Zeinab Mirzaei-Kalar: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Zahra Kiani Nejad: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Resources. Ali Akbar Khandar: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
